mappings.rationaleAI: The IVC's urban planning at Harappa and Mohenjo-daro (B.2.a), standardized measures, trade with Mesopotamia, metallurgy (B.2.b), and undeciphered script (B.2.c) directly address the Indus civilization's social, economic, and cultural developments.
Ashoka's consolidation of the Mauryan empire, conversion to Buddhism, rock and pillar edicts, and governance reforms directly address Asoka's reign and the Mauryan empire (B.5.a).
Alexander's invasion of northwestern India (327 BC) and establishment of Greek settlements directly address the pre-Mauryan period's Greek invasion (B.4.b).

The Gupta Empire's rule of northern India, Hindu religious consolidation, and cultural efflorescence in mathematics, astronomy, and literature directly address the Guptas of North India (C.1) including expansion, Huna invasions, and successor states.
The Neolithic Revolution's polycentric origins including West Asia relate to Late Stone Age hunters and Neolithic settlement in Baluchistan and the Indus Valley (B.1).
The Achaemenid Empire's extension to the Indus Valley provides context for pre-Mauryan states and foreign rulers in the Indian subcontinent (B.4).
Ancient Greece's Hellenistic period and Alexander's conquests reaching India relate to the invasion by Alexander and establishment of Greek settlements (B.4.b).
Stone Age archaeological periodization including Neolithic developments relates to Late Stone Age hunters and Neolithic settlement in the Indus Valley (B.1).
The Silk Road's trade routes connecting South Asia with other regions relate to South Indian maritime trade with the West (B.7.a) and broader trade networks.
Agricultural origins on the Indian subcontinent relate to Neolithic settlement in Baluchistan (B.1) and Indus civilization developments in agriculture and animal husbandry (B.2.b).
Pottery as one of humanity's oldest technologies relates to metalwork and pottery developments in the Indus civilization (B.2.b).
Sumer's trade networks including connections to the Indus Valley are directly relevant to Indus civilization trade developments (B.2.b).
The post-classical period's coverage of South Asian kingdoms and the spread of Buddhism provides context for North India, Deccan, and South India (C, D) from the Guptas through 1200.
The Himalayas' role in South Asian geography and their sacred significance to Hinduism and Buddhism relate to the geography of the Indian subcontinent (A).
Indian art history including patronage of religious art and literature (B.7.b), Pallava developments in religious art and architecture (C.3), and literature and the visual arts (D.3).

The ancient period's coverage of South Asia including the Indus Valley and early religions provides broad context for Indian civilizations' character (A) and prehistoric through classical development (B).
